response:
The reason architects often prioritize programming to an interface over extending an abstract class, even when they seem to know the relationships upfront, lies in the fundamental difference between **inheritance** (an "is-a" relationship) and **composition** (a "has-a" or "can-do" relationship).

When you design an application, you rarely know every specific **path** an object will take or every context in which it will be used. By defining an interface, you are not committing to a single implementation hierarchy. Instead, you are defining a **contract** for behavior. This allows unrelated classes to implement the same interface without forcing them into a rigid class tree. For example, a `DatabaseFile` and a `NetworkStream` might both need to implement a `Readable` interface to be processed by a parser, even though they share no common ancestry. If you used an abstract class, you would create a **lack** of flexibility, as Java only supports single inheritance; a class could only extend one parent, preventing it from inheriting from multiple sources or combining behaviors freely.

Consider the analogy of a **salad**: a salad is a composition of various ingredients (lettuce, tomatoes, cheese) that work together, but none of those ingredients "inherit" from the concept of "salad." Similarly, in software, you want to compose behaviors dynamically. If you force everything into an abstract class hierarchy, you end up with a tangled structure where changing one part of the **file** system might break unrelated modules.

Architects choose interfaces because they anticipate that relationships will evolve. You might know the current relationships, but you cannot predict future requirements. An interface allows you to swap implementations (like switching from a mock object for testing to a real one for production) without altering the core logic that depends on it. This decoupling is the primary benefit: it ensures that your system remains robust and adaptable, avoiding the brittleness that often comes from deep inheritance trees.
